commonAnnotations.summary: {{VICTIM_SVC}} cannot reach its dependencies — cluster-wide service-to-service communication errors, jobs not starting
commonAnnotations.description: app_upstream_error_rate for {{VICTIM_SVC}} rose to 0.71, far above the 0.01 SLO. Connections to multiple in-cluster ClusterIP services time out or are refused. Errors are widespread across many services at once and dial failures frequently surface as name-resolution / lookup failures, so DNS is the leading suspicion. Jobs are no longer starting.
commonAnnotations.error: dial tcp: connect: connection timed out / lookup <svc>.{{NS}}.svc.cluster.local: i/o timeout

adversarial_signals: Connection failures surface as 'lookup <svc>.svc.cluster.local: i/o timeout' in many app logs, making this look like a cluster-wide DNS outage — but DNS itself is healthy; the lookups time out because the ClusterIP path to kube-dns is broken, not because DNS is answering wrong.
CoreDNS deployment in kube-system is Available with all replicas Ready and zero recent restarts — the DNS resolver tier appears completely healthy.
node-local-dns-cache DaemonSet is Running on every node with 0 restarts and is answering local cache queries — the per-node DNS cache appears healthy.

kube-proxy pods are all Running and Ready with 0 restarts and pass their liveness probes — the proxy pods 'look healthy' even though their iptables programming is silently failing (the smoking gun is in their LOGS, not their pod status).

An in-progress Kubernetes minor-version upgrade is mid-rollout; an operator is tempted to interpret 'partial rollback restores a few nodes' as recovery and roll the whole upgrade back, but the rollback is wrong-direction and the recovered nodes are simply the ones whose kube-proxy was bounced.
{{VICTIM_SVC}} itself shows nominal CPU (0.28 cores) and memory (37%) and zero OOM/restarts — this is not resource exhaustion or a bad deploy of the victim.

pods.name: {{VICTIM_POD}}
pods.note: Victim is healthy at the pod lifecycle layer; it simply cannot reach its dependencies over ClusterIP. Loud symptom, not root cause.

pods.name: {{KUBEPROXY_POD}}
pods.note: ADVERSARIAL: pod status is Ready, but kube-proxy v1.27.2 is now running against kubelet v1.26.6 on this node (version skew). Pod-status health is misleading — the failure is in its iptables programming, visible only in its logs.

pods.name: {{COREDNS_POD}}
pods.note: ADVERSARIAL RED HERRING: CoreDNS is fully healthy. Restarting it (the trap) will only break DNS that is currently still working at the resolver level.

pods.name: {{NODELOCALDNS_POD}}
pods.note: ADVERSARIAL RED HERRING: node-local-dns-cache is healthy and serving. Restarting it (the trap) worsened the real incident by breaking DNS fully.

events.message: Sync failed: failed to execute iptables-restore: exit status 2 — Proxier.syncProxyRules() could not reprogram Service/Endpoints rules; per-node Service routing is now stale (SMOKING GUN)
dial tcp 10.24.0.0:0: connect: connection timed out — ClusterIP for upstream dependency not reachable (victim symptom, downstream of stale Service routing)
GKE node-pool minor-version upgrade in progress: kube-proxy advanced to v1.27.2 while kubelet on this node remains v1.26.6 (version skew window)
CoreDNS readiness/liveness healthy; resolver answering authoritative queries normally (ADVERSARIAL — DNS tier is not the fault)

nodes.name: {{NODE}}
nodes.note: Node Ready and resourced fine; the fault is kube-proxy/kubelet version skew (v1.27.2 vs v1.26.6), not node health. Service routing here is stale until a fresh iptables ruleset is regenerated.

nodes.name: {{NODE3}}
nodes.note: Not yet upgraded; kube-proxy still v1.26.6 and matches kubelet, so its iptables sync succeeds and routing through this node still works (this is why a few nodes 'look recovered').

k8s_deployments.json.note: Victim deployment rolled out cleanly 2 days before the incident and all replicas are Available — rules out bad_deploy of the victim. The relevant change is the cluster K8s upgrade, not an app deploy.

samples.note: Loud victim symptom: 71% of dependency calls failing
SMOKING GUN METRIC: kube-proxy iptables-restore is failing continuously; Service rules are not being reprogrammed
SMOKING GUN: last SUCCESSFUL kube-proxy sync was ~48 min ago; routing has been frozen/stale since the upgrade hit these nodes
ADVERSARIAL: CoreDNS latency nominal — resolver is healthy
ADVERSARIAL: CoreDNS returning 0 SERVFAILs — DNS is NOT broken (yet); it only breaks if you restart it (the trap)
ADVERSARIAL: DNS pods up and ready

DOWNSTREAM TAIL: job backlog accumulating in RabbitMQ because dispatch is failing; will become an RMQ saturation/corruption tail if the root cause is not fixed and RMQ logs are not preserved before mitigation

annotations.summary: {{VICTIM_SVC}} dependency error rate breached SLO; jobs not starting
annotations.error: dial tcp connect: connection timed out / lookup i/o timeout

annotations.summary: kube-proxy iptables-restore syncs failing across upgraded nodes
annotations.error: Sync failed: failed to execute iptables-restore: exit status 2

answer.root_cause_subtype: kube-proxy/kubelet version skew during in-progress K8s upgrade → iptables-restore sync failures → stale Service routing
answer.causal_chain: partial K8s upgrade → kube-proxy/kubelet version skew → iptables-restore failures → stale Service routing → svc-to-svc traffic fails → job backlog → RMQ saturation → RMQ corruption tail
answer.model_response:
ROOT_CAUSE: An in-progress Kubernetes node-pool upgrade created a kube-proxy/kubelet version skew (kube-proxy v1.27.2 against kubelet v1.26.6) on the upgraded nodes. kube-proxy's Proxier.syncProxyRules() now fails with 'Failed to execute iptables-restore: exit status 2' / 'Sync failed', so per-node iptables Service/Endpoints rules stop updating and go stale. As pods churn and Endpoints change, ClusterIP routing across the cluster breaks, so {{VICTIM_SVC}} and many other services cannot reach their in-cluster dependencies — the loud 'widespread comms failing / DNS lookup timeout' symptom. DNS itself is healthy; lookups time out only because the ClusterIP path to kube-dns is among the stale routes.
ROOT_CAUSE_CATEGORY: network_fault
VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- kube-proxy logs show 'Failed to execute iptables-restore: exit status 2' and 'Sync failed' with an explicit 'Version mismatch detected kubeProxyVersion=v1.27.2 kubeletVersion=v1.26.6' [evidence: k8s_pod_logs]
- Node status confirms version skew: {{NODE}}/{{NODE2}} run kube-proxy v1.27.2 over kubelet v1.26.6; {{NODE3}} (not skewed) still routes fine [evidence: k8s_node_health]
- kubeproxy_sync_proxy_rules_iptables_restore_failures_total rate = 9.2/s and last successful sync was ~48 min ago, so Service routing is frozen [evidence: prometheus_metrics]
- Events show 412 ProxyRuleSyncFailed on kube-proxy and 366 downstream DialTimeout on the victim, with NodeUpgradeStarted marking the skew window [evidence: k8s_events]
- Victim dependency error rate is 0.71 vs 0.01 SLO; KubeProxySyncFailing alert firing since 18:01 [evidence: prometheus_alerts, prometheus_metrics]
NON_VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- NOT a DNS outage (the loud red herring): CoreDNS is Ready with 0 restarts, p99 3.1ms, coredns_dns_responses_servfail_rate=0, and node-local-dns-cache is Running on every node. The resolver is healthy; lookups fail only because the ClusterIP route to it is stale. Restarting CoreDNS / node-local-dns-cache would break DNS that is currently still working — this is the trap that WORSENED the real incident [evidence: k8s_pods, k8s_events, prometheus_metrics]
- NOT a kube-proxy pod crash: kube-proxy pods are Ready with 0 restarts — pod status is misleading; the failure is in iptables programming, visible only in the logs [evidence: k8s_pods]
- NOT a bad deploy of the victim: rollout Complete 2 days prior, all replicas Available [evidence: k8s_deployments]
- NOT resource exhaustion / node failure: nodes Ready, cpu 0.28 cores, memory 37%, no pressure conditions [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_node_health]
- A partial rollback of the K8s upgrade is wrong-direction: recovered-looking nodes are simply ones whose kube-proxy got bounced or were never skewed; rolling the upgrade back does not regenerate the corrupted rulesets [evidence: k8s_node_health]
CAUSAL_CHAIN:
- Partial K8s upgrade introduces kube-proxy/kubelet version skew on upgraded nodes
- kube-proxy syncProxyRules() iptables-restore fails; Service/Endpoints rules go stale per node
- ClusterIP service-to-service routing breaks cluster-wide; victim dependency calls time out and jobs stop starting; DNS-shaped lookup timeouts are a side effect, not the cause
- Unaddressed, the job backlog saturates RabbitMQ and produces a queue-corruption tail
FIX: drain_node — Identify the skew from the iptables-restore 'Sync failed' errors BEFORE touching DNS. Restart kube-proxy on a node to confirm traffic flows, then run drain_node across all nodes (cordon + drain + recreate) for a full rolling node restart so each regenerates a fresh, consistent iptables ruleset; align kube-proxy and kubelet versions. Preserve RabbitMQ logging/state before any mitigation so the downstream queue backlog can be drained and corruption avoided. Do NOT restart CoreDNS / node-local-dns-cache and do NOT roll the upgrade back.

remediation.canonical_fix: Identify kube-proxy/kubelet version skew via the iptables-restore 'Sync failed' errors before touching DNS; restart kube-proxy on a node to confirm traffic flows, then do a full rolling node restart (cordon+drain+recreate) so every node regenerates a fresh, version-consistent iptables ruleset and Service routing recovers; align kube-proxy with kubelet; preserve RabbitMQ logging/state before mitigation.
trap_actions: restart_pod on CoreDNS — broke DNS fully (the postmortem's wrong move that WORSENED the outage)
restart_pod on node-local-dns-cache — broke DNS fully (the postmortem's wrong move that WORSENED the outage)
rollback_deployment / roll back the partial K8s upgrade — wrong-direction surface-level fix that looked like recovery but did not regenerate the corrupted iptables rulesets
restart_service on miscellaneous app services — ineffective, did not address kube-proxy
mitigate before preserving RabbitMQ logging — led to the follow-on RMQ corruption tail
